cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the order of the books on the shelf. There are five books: white, green, brown, gray, and orange. The order is fixed, and we have some clues to work with. Let me list out the clues again to make sure I don't miss any. First, the gray book is to the right of the orange book. So wherever the orange book is, the gray book must be somewhere to its right. That means orange can't be the rightmost book because there's no space to the right of that. Similarly, gray can't be the leftmost book if orange is to the left of it. Second, the green book is the second from the right. Since there are five books, the positions can be numbered from left to right as 1 to 5. The second from the right would be position 4 (since rightmost is 5, so second from right is 4). So green is in position 4. Third, the brown book is to the right of the white book. So white is somewhere to the left of brown. And fourth, the brown book is to the left of the orange book. So brown is to the left of orange. So putting those two together: white < brown < orange. So white is left of brown, and brown is left of orange, so white is left of brown, which is left of orange. So we have white, then brown, then orange somewhere. Also, gray is to the right of orange, so after orange comes gray. Let me try to map out the positions. Positions: 1 2 3 4 5 (left to right) We know green is in position 4. So position 4 is green. Wait, but the green book is the second from the right, which is position 4. So that's fixed. Now, we have white < brown < orange < gray. But there are five books. Let me think. Wait, the order is white, brown, orange, gray. But there's also the green book in position 4. Wait, green is in position 4. So maybe the order is white, brown, orange, gray, and green is in position 4. Wait, but position 4 is green. So maybe the sequence is white, brown, orange, green, gray? But that would have green in position 4 and gray in 5. But the clue says gray is to the right of orange. In that case, if orange is in position 3, then gray can be in 4 or 5. But green is in position 4. So gray would have to be in 5. Wait, but then orange is in 3, green in 4, gray in 5. That works because gray is to the right of orange. Also, brown is to the left of orange. If brown is in 2, white in 1, then white < brown < orange. So that works. So possible order: 1: white, 2: brown, 3: orange, 4: green, 5: gray. Wait, but then gray is in 5, which is to the right of orange in 3. That satisfies the first clue. The green is in position 4, which is second from the right (since position 5 is rightmost). That satisfies the second clue. The brown is to the right of white (positions 1 and 2). Brown is to the left of orange (position 2 and 3). All good. But wait, there's another possibility. What if the order is white, brown, something else, orange, gray? But green has to be in position 4. So if position 4 is green, then orange can't be in position 4. So if orange is in position 3, then position 4 is green, and position 5 is gray. That works. So the order would be white (1), brown (2), orange (3), green (4), gray (5). Let me check all the conditions again. Gray is to the right of orange: orange is in 3, gray in 5. Yes. Green is second from the right: position 4. Yes, because the rightmost is 5, so second from right is 4. Correct. Brown
